story:
The gavel cracks through a raucous courtroom in Nursery Rhyme Village, where giggles ripple, peanuts pass, and someone hums Baa, Baa, Black Sheep. An owly judge restores order long enough to address a side fiasco: Mrs. Farmer’s overzealous response to escaped lab mice. He has her surrender the carving knife and directs the bailiff to secure the area so the hearing can proceed safely. Then he turns to the main case.

Mr. Wolfe, the prosecutor in an ill-fitting suit and a low growl, resumes questioning the witness: a firm school marm who insists her personal name is “unrecorded.” She testifies that a student’s little lamb violated school rules by coming to class. Asked to identify the culprit, she points toward the defendant’s table, noting that many in the crowded courtroom look similar—the fleece is white as snow, much like the prosecutor’s suit and some of the “peers” in the jury box. To settle the matter, Mr. Wolfe requests a simple test: Mary, the student seated beside the accused, is asked to cross the room. She rises and walks; the lamb follows close behind, confirming its identity.

When Mr. Wolfe calls the lamb a “devious character,” the defense objects to the smear; the judge sustains, and the prosecutor reframes his question. The school marm explains the rule: no animals in class unless used for science; otherwise they distract from learning. Giggles bubble up from the gallery again; the gavel answers with a stern Order. The witness recounts that she turned the lamb out, yet it lingered near until Mary returned, at which point the lamb rejoined her. The implication: Mary has since kept it from disrupting school.

Mr. Wolfe sums up: a wayward lamb interrupted instruction and stole valuable learning time, and should be kept from repeating the offense—he even volunteers to take the animal into custody. The judge declines and calls on the defense.

Mr. Shepherd, a gentle figure in white robe and sandals, lifts the lamb and addresses the bench. He calls it one of his lost sheep—once strayed, now found—draws a line between the lamb’s loyalty to Mary and Mary’s own faithful following of him, and declares that its debt is paid and its fault forgiven. He urges the court to do likewise. The lamb’s misadventure, he suggests, has taught a living lesson to children in the classroom and to the peanut gallery alike: about guidance, repentance, and belonging to a caring fold.

The judge turns to the jury. In cheerful unison they find the defendant not guilty. The courtroom spills forward to encircle the Good Shepherd. Gavel down: Case dismissed. Order, for once, holds—tinged with relief, a lesson learned, and a bit of Nursery Rhyme music still humming under the breath of a village that remembers both rules and mercy.
